question:
1. Let \( x^{(n)}=x(x-1) \cdots(x-n+1) \) for \( n \) a positive integer and let \( x^{(0)} \) \( =1 \). Prove that
\[
(x+y)^{(n)}=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k} x^{(k)} y^{(n-k)}
\]

Note:
\[
\binom{n}{k}=\frac{n(n-1) \cdots(n-k+1)}{1 \cdot 2 \cdots k}
\]
solution:
First Solution. We use induction on \( n \). The required equation is clearly valid for \( n=0 \). Suppose it is true for \( n=p \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(x+y)^{(p+1)} & =(x+y)^{(p)}(x+y-p) \\
& =\sum_{k=0}^{p}\binom{p}{k} x^{(k)} y^{(p-k)}(x-k+y-(p-k)) \\
& =\sum_{k=0}^{p}\binom{p}{k} x^{(k+1)} y^{(p-k)}+\sum_{k=0}^{p}\binom{p}{k} x^{(k)} y^{(p-k+1)} \\
& =\sum_{k=1}^{p+1}\binom{p}{k-1} x^{(k)} y^{(p+1-k)}+\sum_{k=0}^{p}\binom{p}{k} x^{(k)} y^{(p+1-k)} \\
& \left.=\sum_{k=0}^{p+1}\left|\binom{p}{k-1}+\binom{p}{k}\right| x^{(k)} y^{(p+1} \quad k\right) \\
& =\sum_{k=0}^{p+1}\binom{p+1}{k} x^{(k)} y^{(p+1-k)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus it is also true for \( n=p+1 \). This completes the induction.
Second Solution. We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(1+t)^{x}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{(k)}}{k!} t^{k} \\
(1+t)^{y}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{y^{(k)}}{k!} t^{k}
\end{array}
\]
for \( |t|<1 \) and all \( x, y \). Multiplying these series we obtain
\[
(1+t)^{x+y}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{x^{(k)} y^{(n k)}}{k!(n-k)!} t^{n}
\]
for \( |t|<1 \). But we know
\[
(1+t)^{x+y}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(x+y)^{(n)}}{n!} t^{n}
\]

Since power series representations are unique, we have
\[
\frac{(x+y)^{(n)}}{n!}=\sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{x^{(k)} y^{(n-k)}}{k!(n-k)!}
\]
which is equivalent to the required identity.

descriptive_long.question:
1. Let \( variablex^{(integern)}=variablex(variablex-1) \cdots(variablex-integern+1) \) for \( integern \) a positive integer and let \( variablex^{(0)} =1 \). Prove that
\[
(variablex+variabley)^{(integern)}=\sum_{indexk=0}^{integern}\binom{integern}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integern-indexk)}
\]

Note:
\[
\binom{integern}{indexk}=\frac{integern(integern-1) \cdots(integern-indexk+1)}{1 \cdot 2 \cdots indexk}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. We use induction on \( integern \). The required equation is clearly valid for \( integern=0 \). Suppose it is true for \( integern=integerp \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(variablex+variabley)^{(integerp+1)} & =(variablex+variabley)^{(integerp)}(variablex+variabley-integerp) \\
& =\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp}\binom{integerp}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp-indexk)}(variablex-indexk+variabley-(integerp-indexk)) \\
& =\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp}\binom{integerp}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk+1)} variabley^{(integerp-indexk)}+\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp}\binom{integerp}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp-indexk+1)} \\
& =\sum_{indexk=1}^{integerp+1}\binom{integerp}{indexk-1} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp+1-indexk)}+\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp}\binom{integerp}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp+1-indexk)} \\
& =\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp+1}\left(\binom{integerp}{indexk-1}+\binom{integerp}{indexk}\right) variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp+1-indexk)} \\
& =\sum_{indexk=0}^{integerp+1}\binom{integerp+1}{indexk} variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integerp+1-indexk)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus it is also true for \( integern=integerp+1 \). This completes the induction.

Second Solution. We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(1+auxiliaryt)^{variablex}=\sum_{indexk=0}^{\infty} \frac{variablex^{(indexk)}}{indexk!} auxiliaryt^{indexk} \\
(1+auxiliaryt)^{variabley}=\sum_{indexk=0}^{\infty} \frac{variabley^{(indexk)}}{indexk!} auxiliaryt^{indexk}
\end{array}
\]
for \( |auxiliaryt|<1 \) and all \( variablex, variabley \). Multiplying these series we obtain
\[
(1+auxiliaryt)^{variablex+variabley}=\sum_{integern=0}^{\infty} \sum_{indexk=0}^{integern} \frac{variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integern-indexk)}}{indexk!(integern-indexk)!} auxiliaryt^{integern}
\]
for \( |auxiliaryt|<1 \). But we know
\[
(1+auxiliaryt)^{variablex+variabley}=\sum_{integern=0}^{\infty} \frac{(variablex+variabley)^{(integern)}}{integern!} auxiliaryt^{integern}
\]

Since power series representations are unique, we have
\[
\frac{(variablex+variabley)^{(integern)}}{integern!}=\sum_{indexk=0}^{integern} \frac{variablex^{(indexk)} variabley^{(integern-indexk)}}{indexk!(integern-indexk)!}
\]
which is equivalent to the required identity.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem:
<<<
1. Let \( meadowlark^{(hatchling)}=meadowlark(meadowlark-1) \cdots(meadowlark-hatchling+1) \) for \( hatchling \) a positive integer and let \( meadowlark^{(0)} \) \( =1 \). Prove that
\[
(meadowlark+turnpike)^{(hatchling)}=\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{hatchling}\binom{hatchling}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(hatchling-sandpiper)}
\]

Note:
\[
\binom{hatchling}{sandpiper}=\frac{hatchling(hatchling-1) \cdots(hatchling-sandpiper+1)}{1 \cdot 2 \cdots sandpiper}
\]
>>>
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution:
<<<
First Solution. We use induction on \( hatchling \). The required equation is clearly valid for \( hatchling=0 \). Suppose it is true for \( hatchling=blacksmith \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(meadowlark+turnpike)^{(blacksmith+1)} & =(meadowlark+turnpike)^{(blacksmith)}(meadowlark+turnpike-blacksmith) \\
& =\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith}\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith-sandpiper)}(meadowlark-sandpiper+turnpike-(blacksmith-sandpiper)) \\
& =\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith}\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper+1)} turnpike^{(blacksmith-sandpiper)}+\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith}\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith-sandpiper+1)} \\
& =\sum_{sandpiper=1}^{blacksmith+1}\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper-1} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith+1-sandpiper)}+\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith}\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith+1-sandpiper)} \\
& =\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith+1}\left|\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper-1}+\binom{blacksmith}{sandpiper}\right| meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith+1-sandpiper)} \\
& =\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{blacksmith+1}\binom{blacksmith+1}{sandpiper} meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(blacksmith+1-sandpiper)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus it is also true for \( hatchling=blacksmith+1 \). This completes the induction.

Second Solution. We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(1+drumstick)^{meadowlark}=\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{\infty} \frac{meadowlark^{(sandpiper)}}{sandpiper!} drumstick^{sandpiper} \\
(1+drumstick)^{turnpike}=\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{\infty} \frac{turnpike^{(sandpiper)}}{sandpiper!} drumstick^{sandpiper}
\end{array}
\]
for \( |drumstick|<1 \) and all \( meadowlark, turnpike \). Multiplying these series we obtain
\[
(1+drumstick)^{meadowlark+turnpike}=\sum_{hatchling=0}^{\infty} \sum_{sandpiper=0}^{hatchling} \frac{meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(hatchling-sandpiper)}}{sandpiper!(hatchling-sandpiper)!} drumstick^{hatchling}
\]
for \( |drumstick|<1 \). But we know
\[
(1+drumstick)^{meadowlark+turnpike}=\sum_{hatchling=0}^{\infty} \frac{(meadowlark+turnpike)^{(hatchling)}}{hatchling!} drumstick^{hatchling}
\]

Since power series representations are unique, we have
\[
\frac{(meadowlark+turnpike)^{(hatchling)}}{hatchling!}=\sum_{sandpiper=0}^{hatchling} \frac{meadowlark^{(sandpiper)} turnpike^{(hatchling-sandpiper)}}{sandpiper!(hatchling-sandpiper)!}
\]
which is equivalent to the required identity.
>>>

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
1. Let \( fixedvalue^{(definite)}=fixedvalue(fixedvalue-1) \cdots(fixedvalue-definite+1) \) for \( definite \) a positive integer and let \( fixedvalue^{(0)} =1 \). Prove that
\[
(fixedvalue+steadyvalue)^{(definite)}=\sum_{outsider=0}^{definite}\binom{definite}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(definite-outsider)}
\]

Note:
\[
\binom{definite}{outsider}=\frac{definite(definite-1) \cdots(definite-outsider+1)}{1 \cdot 2 \cdots outsider}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. We use induction on \( definite \). The required equation is clearly valid for \( definite=0 \). Suppose it is true for \( definite=variable \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(fixedvalue+steadyvalue)^{(variable+1)} & =(fixedvalue+steadyvalue)^{(variable)}(fixedvalue+steadyvalue-variable) \\
& =\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable}\binom{variable}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable-outsider)}(fixedvalue-outsider+steadyvalue-(variable-outsider)) \\
& =\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable}\binom{variable}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider+1)} steadyvalue^{(variable-outsider)}+\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable}\binom{variable}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable-outsider+1)} \\
& =\sum_{outsider=1}^{variable+1}\binom{variable}{outsider-1} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable+1-outsider)}+\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable}\binom{variable}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable+1-outsider)} \\
& \left.=\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable+1}\left|\binom{variable}{outsider-1}+\binom{variable}{outsider}\right| fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable+1} \quad outsider\right) \\
& =\sum_{outsider=0}^{variable+1}\binom{variable+1}{outsider} fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(variable+1-outsider)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus it is also true for \( definite=variable+1 \). This completes the induction.

Second Solution. We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(1+timeless)^{fixedvalue}=\sum_{outsider=0}^{\infty} \frac{fixedvalue^{(outsider)}}{outsider!} timeless^{outsider} \\
(1+timeless)^{steadyvalue}=\sum_{outsider=0}^{\infty} \frac{steadyvalue^{(outsider)}}{outsider!} timeless^{outsider}
\end{array}
\]
for \( |timeless|<1 \) and all \( fixedvalue, steadyvalue \). Multiplying these series we obtain
\[
(1+timeless)^{fixedvalue+steadyvalue}=\sum_{definite=0}^{\infty} \sum_{outsider=0}^{definite} \frac{fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(definite-outsider)}}{outsider!(definite-outsider)!} timeless^{definite}
\]
for \( |timeless|<1 \). But we know
\[
(1+timeless)^{fixedvalue+steadyvalue}=\sum_{definite=0}^{\infty} \frac{(fixedvalue+steadyvalue)^{(definite)}}{definite!} timeless^{definite}
\]

Since power series representations are unique, we have
\[
\frac{(fixedvalue+steadyvalue)^{(definite)}}{definite!}=\sum_{outsider=0}^{definite} \frac{fixedvalue^{(outsider)} steadyvalue^{(definite-outsider)}}{outsider!(definite-outsider)!}
\]
which is equivalent to the required identity.

garbled_string.question:
1. Let \( qzxwvtnp^{(fedcbazy)}=qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp-1) \cdots(qzxwvtnp-fedcbazy+1) \) for \( fedcbazy \) a positive integer and let \( qzxwvtnp^{(0)} =1 \). Prove that
\[
(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)^{(fedcbazy)}=\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{fedcbazy}\binom{fedcbazy}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(fedcbazy-plokijuh)}
\]

Note:
\[
\binom{fedcbazy}{plokijuh}=\frac{fedcbazy(fedcbazy-1) \cdots(fedcbazy-plokijuh+1)}{1 \cdot 2 \cdots plokijuh}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. We use induction on \( fedcbazy \). The required equation is clearly valid for \( fedcbazy=0 \). Suppose it is true for \( fedcbazy=lkjhgfds \). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)^{(lkjhgfds+1)} & =(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)^{(lkjhgfds)}(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla-lkjhgfds) \
& =\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds}\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds-plokijuh)}(qzxwvtnp-plokijuh+hjgrksla-(lkjhgfds-plokijuh)) \
& =\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds}\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh+1)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds-plokijuh)}+\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds}\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds-plokijuh+1)} \
& =\sum_{plokijuh=1}^{lkjhgfds+1}\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh-1} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds+1-plokijuh)}+\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds}\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds+1-plokijuh)} \
& \left.=\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds+1}\left|\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh-1}+\binom{lkjhgfds}{plokijuh}\right| qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds+1} \quad plokijuh\right) \
& =\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{lkjhgfds+1}\binom{lkjhgfds+1}{plokijuh} qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(lkjhgfds+1-plokijuh)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus it is also true for \( fedcbazy=lkjhgfds+1 \). This completes the induction.

Second Solution. We have
\[
\begin{array}{l}
(1+mnbvcxza)^{qzxwvtnp}=\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{\infty} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)}}{plokijuh!} mnbvcxza^{plokijuh} \\
(1+mnbvcxza)^{hjgrksla}=\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{\infty} \frac{hjgrksla^{(plokijuh)}}{plokijuh!} mnbvcxza^{plokijuh}
\end{array}
\]
for \( |mnbvcxza|<1 \) and all \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \). Multiplying these series we obtain
\[
(1+mnbvcxza)^{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla}=\sum_{fedcbazy=0}^{\infty} \sum_{plokijuh=0}^{fedcbazy} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(fedcbazy plokijuh)}}{plokijuh!(fedcbazy-plokijuh)!} mnbvcxza^{fedcbazy}
\]
for \( |mnbvcxza|<1 \). But we know
\[
(1+mnbvcxza)^{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla}=\sum_{fedcbazy=0}^{\infty} \frac{(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)^{(fedcbazy)}}{fedcbazy!} mnbvcxza^{fedcbazy}
\]

Since power series representations are unique, we have
\[
\frac{(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)^{(fedcbazy)}}{fedcbazy!}=\sum_{plokijuh=0}^{fedcbazy} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{(plokijuh)} hjgrksla^{(fedcbazy-plokijuh)}}{plokijuh!(fedcbazy-plokijuh)!}
\]
which is equivalent to the required identity.

kernel_variant.question:
Let (z)_0 := 1 and, for any positive integer t,
\[(z)_t := z\,(z-1)\cdots(z-t+1).\]
For commuting symbols a and b and each integer m \ge 1 prove the identity
\[
(a+b)_m\;=\;\sum_{r=0}^{m}\binom{m}{r}\, (a)_r\,(b)_{m-r}.
\]
(The usual binomial coefficient \(\binom{m}{r}=\dfrac{m(m-1)\cdots(m-r+1)}{r!}\) is used.)
kernel_variant.solution:
We induct on the order $m$ of the falling factorial.

Base case $m = 1$.  
The right-hand side equals
  \[(a)_0(b)_1 + (a)_1(b)_0 = b + a = a+b = (a+b)_1,\]
so the formula is valid for $m = 1$.

Induction hypothesis.  
Assume that for some fixed integer $s \ge 1$ we have
  \[(a+b)_s = \sum_{r=0}^{s}\binom{s}{r}(a)_r\,(b)_{s-r}.\]

Inductive step (from $s$ to $s+1$).  
Using the elementary identity $(z)_t\,(z-t) = (z)_{t+1}$ we write
  \[
  (a+b)_{s+1} = (a+b)_s\,(a+b-s).
  \]
Insert the inductive sum for $(a+b)_s$ and split the factor $a+b-s$:
  \[
  \begin{aligned}
  (a+b)_{s+1}
     &= \sum_{r=0}^{s}\binom{s}{r}(a)_r\,(b)_{s-r}\,\bigl[(a-r)+(b-(s-r))\bigr]\\[2pt]
     &= \sum_{r=0}^{s}\binom{s}{r}(a)_{r+1}(b)_{s-r}
        + \sum_{r=0}^{s}\binom{s}{r}(a)_r(b)_{s-r+1}.
  \end{aligned}
  \]
Raise the index $r$ in the first sum (let $r\mapsto r-1$) to obtain two sums indexed from $0$ to $s+1$:
  \[
  \begin{aligned}
  (a+b)_{s+1}
     &= \sum_{r=1}^{s+1}\binom{s}{r-1}(a)_r(b)_{s+1-r}
        + \sum_{r=0}^{s}\binom{s}{r}(a)_r(b)_{s+1-r}.
  \end{aligned}
  \]
Combine the two sums term-wise; for $0\le r\le s+1$ the coefficient of $(a)_r(b)_{s+1-r}$ is
  \[\binom{s}{r-1}+\binom{s}{r}=\binom{s+1}{r}\]
by Pascal's identity, interpreting $\binom{s}{-1}=0=\binom{s}{s+1}$.  Therefore
  \[
  (a+b)_{s+1}=\sum_{r=0}^{s+1}\binom{s+1}{r}(a)_r(b)_{s+1-r}),
  \]
which is precisely the required statement with $m = s+1$.  The induction is complete.

Hence the identity holds for every integer $m \ge 1$.

core_steps: Verify the identity for the base case n = 0.
Assume it is true for n = p (inductive hypothesis).
Express (x + y)^{(p+1)} as (x + y)^{(p)}·(x + y − p) and replace (x + y)^{(p)} by the inductive sum.
Use the rule x^{(k)}(x − k) = x^{(k+1)} to raise the falling-factorial order and shift indices.
Combine the two resulting sums via Pascal’s identity C(p,k−1)+C(p,k)=C(p+1,k) to obtain the required formula, completing the induction.

slot1.description: Choice of letters for the two addends—the proof works with any symbols that commute under multiplication/addition.

slot2.description: Notation for the falling factorial; any symbol (e.g., (x)_n, P_n(x)) that satisfies (x)^{(n)}(x−n) = (x)^{(n+1)} may be substituted.

slot3.description: Names of the summation/induction indices; any distinct letters can replace n, k, p.

slot4.description: Location of the base case—one may start the induction at n = 1 instead of n = 0 with no change in logic.
